title: A Neural-Network Framework to Learn History-Dependent Constitutive Laws and Identifiability of Internal Variables
authors: Mayank Raj, Lianghao Cao, Andrew Stuart, Kaushik Bhattacharya
abstract: The identification of constitutive laws is ubiquitous in engineering: in modeling of materials where experimental data are fitted to mathematical models or learning surrogate models to beat the FE\textsuperscript{2} computational cost of multiscale numerical simulations. However, these models of constitutive laws, unless equipped with a potential formulation, are not necessarily consistent with (a) the second law of thermodynamics; (b) stability of the material under extreme applied strain; and (c) the mathematical theory underpinning the existence of solutions of the governing equation. In this work, we present a causal and energetic formulation, consistent with aforementioned properties, of learning a history-dependent constitutive law. This characterization of the class of internal variables sheds light on the equivalence class of equivalent surrogate models for the constitutive law. We show that the internal variables that are learned from the data are unique up to a linear transform. The framework is deployed to learn the Taylor-averaged response of a polycrystalline magnesium unit cell. We achieve 2\% relative error in the prediction of the Taylor-averaged response.
summary: This paper introduces a new neural network architecture designed to learn material constitutive laws while strictly adhering to thermodynamic and mathematical stability requirements. It successfully predicts the mechanical response of magnesium with high accuracy and provides a theoretical proof that the learned internal variables are unique up to a linear transform.
main_problem: Developing a neural network framework to learn history-dependent constitutive laws that satisfy fundamental physical constraints like the second law of thermodynamics, polyconvexity, and material stability, while addressing the identifiability of learned internal variables.
main_result: The authors present a physically consistent framework that achieves 2% relative error in predicting the response of magnesium and theoretically prove that learned internal variables are unique up to a linear transformation.
method: The framework uses Input Convex Neural Networks (ICNN) to approximate energy and dissipation potentials, incorporating growth functions to enforce stability and a hydrostatic-deviatoric decomposition to improve accuracy.
model_or_system: The system is a polycrystalline magnesium (Mg) unit cell modeled via Taylor-averaged response, exhibiting viscoplastic, finite-deformation, and non-Markovian (history-dependent) behavior.
key_observables: Deformation gradient tensor (F) and first Piola-Kirchhoff stress tensor (P).
important_parameters: Number of internal variables (n=6 found to be optimal), growth/singularity parameters (kappa, Xi), and transition rate (alpha).
main_assumptions: The material response is assumed to be viscoplastic; the converse theorem for identifiability requires specific reachability conditions (Assumption 4.9) and sufficient dimensionality (m >= n).
figures_summary: Figures show time-series of stress and strain, distribution of stress components, error analysis regarding the number of internal variables and energy decomposition, and a visual proof of internal variable identifiability via linear regression.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the need for physically consistent models to introducing a Markovian framework with internal variables, followed by detailing the neural network architecture and training, demonstrating application to magnesium, and concluding with mathematical proofs of identifiability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
